weakestLink.whatsRight: DLC introduces a fundamentally different risk profile. Air cooling fails gracefully — temperatures rise slowly when a CRAH fails, giving minutes to hours to respond. Liquid cooling can fail catastrophically — a pump failure or major leak can cause thermal throttling in seconds and potential hardware damage in minutes. Operations teams need specific training, rehearsed emergency procedures, and 24/7 monitoring integration before the system goes live.
howToGetThere: Step 1: Develop a DLC-specific operations manual before commissioning — covering normal operation, alarm response, maintenance procedures, coolant management, and emergency shutdown (indicative: €15,000–25,000 for a specialist to develop, Irish market 2025)

Step 2: Train all operations staff on liquid cooling systems — minimum 3-day specialist training per team member covering system operation, leak response, and coolant handling (indicative: €2,000–3,000 per person)

Step 3: Integrate CDU monitoring into the existing BMS/DCIM platform — flow rate, supply/return temperature, pressure, and leak detection status must be visible in the same interface as existing cooling systems
Step 4: Conduct a tabletop exercise simulating a major coolant leak during peak load — test the team's response time and decision-making before the system goes live

Step 5: Establish a coolant quality monitoring programme — quarterly sampling (pH, conductivity, glycol %, microbial count) with defined action thresholds (indicative: €3,000–5,000/year for 50-rack zone)

rhetoric.asset_management: The capex for a 50-rack DLC zone at 100 kW is approximately €4–6M all-in (CDUs, piping, structural, electrical, commissioning — indicative, Irish market 2025). The revenue from 50 racks at 100 kW with AI tenant premium pricing is approximately €4.5–6.0M per year. Payback on the infrastructure investment: under 12 months if the racks are filled. The risk is not the technology — it is tenant commitment. A binding letter of intent from the hyperscaler before approving capex de-risks the investment.
rhetoric.technology: For a 50-rack zone at 100 kW, the DLC system architecture is: 5 CDUs (4 active + 1 standby) at 500 kW each, DN100 supply and return headers running overhead, rack manifolds with individual server isolation valves, water-glycol coolant at 30°C supply / 40°C return, and leak detection cable under every row. Total coolant volume: approximately 4,000 litres. The system connects to the existing chilled water loop on the primary side — no new chillers required if the current plant has capacity for the additional 2 MW reject heat plus CDU pump energy.
rhetoric.technical: Flow measurement at the CDU shows 52.6 l/s total secondary flow for 5 MW of heat rejection at 10°C ΔT across water-glycol at 3.8 kJ/kg·K. The DN100 header at this flow rate operates at 1.7 m/s — within the ASHRAE-recommended 2 m/s maximum for erosion prevention. Pressure drop across the piping network (25m run, 8 elbows, 50 rack manifold connections): approximately 1.8 bar. CDU pump duty: approximately 55 kW per unit at this pressure and flow, totalling 220 kW for 4 active CDUs. This represents 4.4% of IT load as cooling energy — compared to 35–45% for equivalent air cooling.
rhetoric.compliance: A 100 kW DLC zone achieves approximately 1.08–1.12 PUE for the zone, well within the EU Taxonomy ≤1.3 threshold. Under EED 2023/1791 Article 12, the facility reports a blended PUE across all zones — the DLC zone's low PUE offsets Hall A's higher air-cooled PUE. The facility-wide blended PUE would improve from 1.50 to approximately 1.28, crossing the Taxonomy threshold for the first time. This is a material improvement for SFDR fund reporting — the facility moves from non-aligned to aligned.
rhetoric.cost: Detailed cost build-up for 50-rack DLC zone at 100 kW (indicative, Irish market 2025): CDU units (5 × €130K) = €650K. Piping and manifolds (supply/return headers, rack connections, fittings): €480K. Leak detection and containment: €85K. Structural reinforcement for rack weight (refer DC-AI-003): €380K. Electrical supply for CDUs and controls: €195K. BMS/DCIM integration: €65K. Commissioning and testing: €220K. Operations training and manual development: €45K. Contingency 15%: €312K. Total: approximately €2.43M for DLC-specific infrastructure. Note: this excludes the power distribution upgrades (L7) and any MIC/grid upgrades (L1/DC-AI-004), which are separate cost lines.

scenario.title: DLC System Design for 50-Rack AI Zone
scenario.situation: Clonshaugh Hall B is approved for a 50-rack AI zone at 100 kW/rack. CDUs will be located in a new plant room 20m from the rack zone. The facility has 1.5 MW of spare chilled water capacity on the primary loop. Coolant selected: 25% propylene glycol in deionised water. The operations team has zero prior experience with liquid cooling. Go-live target: 9 months.
scenario.challenge: What are the critical design parameters for the DLC secondary loop, and what operational readiness steps must be completed before the first AI rack is energised?
scenario.fix: Step 1: Size the secondary loop — 50 × 100 kW = 5 MW heat load. At 10°C coolant ΔT, flow = 5,000 / (3.8 × 10) = 131.6 kg/s ≈ 132 l/s. With 5 CDUs at 500 kW (4+1), each handles 33 l/s. Step 2: Size piping — DN100 header at 132 l/s gives 1.7 m/s velocity (within 2 m/s limit). Pressure drop at 20m run with fittings: approximately 1.8 bar. Step 3: CDU pump selection — 33 l/s at 1.8 bar per CDU, approximately 55 kW pump motor. Total CDU electrical demand: 5 × 55 = 275 kW (plus controls), requiring a dedicated 400A supply from the main switchboard. Step 4: Leak containment — install bunded drip trays under all 50 rack positions (capacity: 100 litres each), floor drainage to a 5,000-litre collection sump, with automatic pump-out. Leak detection cable: 250m at €65/m = €16,250. Step 5: Operational readiness — commission operations manual (months 1–3), train 8 operations staff (months 4–5), integrate CDU monitoring into DCIM (months 5–7), conduct leak response tabletop exercise (month 7), complete hot commissioning with progressive load ramp (months 8–9). Critical path item: CDU procurement (12–16 week lead time) — order must be placed by month 1.

title: GB300 Class Density
subtitle: 132–142 kW/rack and beyond — NVIDIA's current ceiling
plainEnglish: The NVIDIA GB300 NVL72 packs 72 GPUs into a single rack drawing 132–142 kW under normal load and peaking at 155 kW. This is not just more of the same — the power density creates cascading constraints in power delivery, structural loading, cooling capacity, and operational risk that are qualitatively different from a 100 kW deployment.
sourceNote: HPE GB300 NVL72 Product Specifications 2025 — T3. Lenovo GB300 NVL72 Product Guide 2026 — T3. TrendForce GB300 Forecast Mar 2025 — T2. Schneider Electric 800 VDC Blog Oct 2025 — T3. Barrack AI infrastructure analysis Mar 2026 — T2.
retrofitRelevance: GB300-class density is the current peak of the market. A retrofit designed for 100 kW/rack may not accommodate 135 kW without further upgrades. Future-proofing decisions made at L5 determine whether a GB300 deployment requires a second round of infrastructure investment.
crossRefs: DC-AI-002 L4 (DLC architecture — 90% liquid / 10% air split for GB300)
DC-AI-003 L1 (Floor loading — GB300 racks at 3,000–3,500 lbs)
DC-AI-004 L2 (Demand growth — GB300 impact on MIC utilisation)
DC-AI-008 L7 (Technology Roadmap — Rubin at 400 kW+)

facts.definition: The electrical power consumption range of a fully configured NVIDIA GB300 NVL72 rack containing 72 Blackwell Ultra GPUs and 36 Grace CPUs: 132–142 kW TDP (thermal design power) with 135 kW typical, peaking at 155 kW under maximum workload. Confirmed by HPE, Lenovo, TrendForce, and Schneider Electric (2025–2026).
facts.plain: One rack of NVIDIA's current top-end AI hardware draws as much power as 17 average server racks — or roughly the same as 50 homes. Under peak load it can spike to 155 kW, which is more power than many small commercial buildings use in total.
facts.number: TDP: 132–142 kW (135 kW typical). Peak: 155 kW. Per GPU: 1,400W (72 GPUs × 1,400W = 100.8 kW GPU-only). Balance: Grace CPUs, NVSwitches, networking, PSUs, fans. Heat split: 90% to liquid, 10% to air.
facts.standard: HPE GB300 NVL72 Product Specifications (2025) — T3. Lenovo Product Guide (2026) — T3. TrendForce (Mar 2025) — T2.
facts.soWhat: At 135 kW typical, a single GB300 rack demands more power than Clonshaugh's entire average per-rack allocation multiplied by 17. Deploying even 10 of these racks requires 1.35 MW — consuming 27% of the site's total 5 MVA MIC.

facts.definition: A dynamic power management feature in the GB300 NVL72 where power is redistributed between GPU compute trays within the rack based on workload phase. Each of the 18 compute trays (4 GPUs + 2 Grace CPUs) draws variable power depending on whether GPUs are in compute, memory-access, or idle phases.
facts.plain: The rack does not draw a steady 135 kW — power surges between different trays as the AI workload shifts between computation and data movement phases. One tray might spike to its maximum while another drops, keeping the rack total within the TDP envelope. This is like an orchestra where different sections play louder at different times — the total volume stays within limits but individual instruments peak and trough.
facts.number: Each compute tray: 4 GPUs × 1,400W + 2 Grace CPUs × 250W = 6,100W peak per tray. 18 trays × 6,100W = 109,800W theoretical maximum. Power sloshing keeps rack total within 135–142 kW by dynamically redistributing between trays. Instantaneous tray-level variation: ±30%.
facts.standard: NVIDIA GB300 NVL72 Reference Architecture (manufacturer — T3). Barrack AI analysis (2026) — T2.
facts.soWhat: Power sloshing means the electrical distribution must handle transient current spikes at individual tray feeds even though the rack-level average stays within TDP. PDUs and circuit protection must be rated for peak, not average — a 135 kW average rack can momentarily demand 155 kW.

facts.definition: The GB300 NVL72 uses a vertical 50V DC busbar running through the rack chassis to distribute power from the power supply units (PSUs) to the compute trays. At 135 kW and 50V, the busbar carries over 2,700 amps.
facts.plain: Inside the rack, power is delivered at 50 volts DC through a copper bar running top to bottom. At 135 kW, this bar carries over 2,700 amps — an enormous current requiring very heavy copper conductors. This is approaching the physical limits of what fits inside a standard 19-inch rack.
facts.number: Current at 50V: I = P/V = 135,000/50 = 2,700A. Busbar cross-section: approximately 2,500 mm² copper to carry this current within temperature limits. Weight of busbar copper alone: approximately 50 kg. 800V DC sidecar PSU demonstrated at GTC 2025 for next-generation Rubin platform (2027).
facts.standard: NVIDIA GB300 NVL72 Reference Architecture (T3). Schneider Electric Blog 'The 1 MW AI IT rack is coming' (Oct 2025) — T3.
facts.soWhat: The 50V DC busbar is at its engineering limit. NVIDIA's demonstration of an 800V DC sidecar PSU at GTC 2025 signals that the next generation (Rubin, 2027) will move to higher voltage distribution to reduce current and conductor size. Facilities designed for GB300 should plan cable pathways and electrical rooms that can accommodate the transition to 800V DC.

facts.definition: The total mass of a fully configured liquid-cooled AI GPU rack including servers, networking, coolant, manifolds, and structural frame. GB300 NVL72 racks weigh approximately 3,000–3,500 lbs (1,360–1,590 kg).
facts.plain: A single GB300 rack, full of GPUs and liquid coolant, weighs as much as a small car. Most data centre raised floors were designed for 150 pounds per square foot — a GB300 rack on standard 600mm × 1200mm tiles exceeds this rating. The floor may literally not be strong enough to hold the rack.
facts.number: GB300 rack: 3,000–3,500 lbs (1,360–1,590 kg). Footprint: approximately 0.72 m² (800mm × 900mm rack + clearance). Point load: approximately 1,890–2,200 lbs/ft² on four levelling feet — far exceeding typical 150 psf raised floor rating.
facts.standard: Manufacturer specifications (NVIDIA, HPE, Lenovo, Supermicro — T3). Structural loading per EN 1991-1-1 (Eurocode 1: Actions on structures).
facts.soWhat: Floor loading is the constraint nobody thinks about until the structural engineer says the racks cannot be placed where planned. At Clonshaugh, the 150 psf raised floor cannot support a GB300 rack without structural reinforcement — either slab-to-slab mounting or localised floor strengthening.

facts.definition: The maximum thermal design power of a single NVIDIA B300 Blackwell Ultra GPU, representing the maximum sustained heat dissipation the cooling system must handle per chip.
facts.plain: Each of the 72 GPUs in a GB300 rack can draw up to 1,400 watts — roughly the same as a large domestic microwave oven running continuously. At this power level, a single GPU generates enough heat to warm a small room. Multiply by 72 and the rack produces heat equivalent to a 100 kW electric heater.
facts.number: B300 GPU TDP: 1,400W. Previous generation B200: 1,000W (40% increase). H100 (2022): 700W. Trajectory: doubling every ~2 years. Projected next-gen (Rubin, 2027): 1,800–2,000W per GPU.
facts.standard: NVIDIA B300 GPU Specifications (manufacturer — T3). NVIDIA GPU roadmap presentations (T3).
facts.soWhat: At 1,400W per GPU, liquid cooling is a hard requirement — no air cooling configuration exists for the GB300. The doubling trajectory means that today's 135 kW rack becomes 200+ kW within one hardware generation. Any retrofit infrastructure must account for this growth or face a second round of upgrades within 3 years.

grammar.whatItLooksLike: Massive rack enclosures (800mm × 1200mm footprint, taller than standard 42U) with multiple thick coolant hoses at the rear. Visible 160A IEC 60309 power connectors — multiple per rack. No traditional server trays visible; instead, compute tray modules that slide in horizontally with blind-mate coolant connectors. A tangible sense of concentrated energy — the rack hums with electrical load. CDU plant room nearby with DN100 piping carrying large volumes of coolant.
siteChecklist: Confirm structural loading capacity at each planned GB300 rack position — 3,000–3,500 lbs point load on approximately 0.72 m² footprint
Verify electrical supply can deliver 155 kW peak per rack position — 3-phase 415V, approximately 250A per rack at 0.85 pf
Confirm CDU capacity scaled for GB300 peak (155 kW × rack count, not TDP) — CDU sizing must use peak, not average
Assess ceiling height — GB300 racks in 800mm × 1200mm cabinets are taller than legacy 600mm × 1000mm; verify clearance with containment and overhead piping
Plan for 800V DC future-proofing — ensure cable pathways and electrical rooms can accommodate a voltage transition within 3 years

causeAndEffect.cause: A facility designs the DLC infrastructure for 100 kW/rack (Level 5 specification) and 12 months later receives a request to deploy GB300 NVL72 racks at 135 kW typical / 155 kW peak in the same zone.
causeAndEffect.effect: The CDU system sized at 500 kW per unit (4+1 for 50 × 100 kW = 5 MW) cannot handle 50 × 135 kW = 6.75 MW. Two additional CDUs are required (cost: approximately €260K plus piping modifications €150K). The DN100 piping headers, designed for 132 l/s at 100 kW density, must now handle 178 l/s — velocity increases to 2.3 m/s, exceeding the 2.0 m/s erosion limit. Piping upsizing to DN125 is required (cost: approximately €280K including re-routing and hall disruption). Total cost of the density increase: approximately €690K — plus 8 weeks of disruption to existing tenants.
causeAndEffect.insight: When designing DLC infrastructure for a retrofit, size piping and CDU for the next density tier, not the current requirement. The marginal cost of DN125 vs DN100 at installation is approximately 15–20%. The cost of upgrading from DN100 to DN125 after deployment is 300–400% of the original piping cost due to system drain, re-route, and recommissioning.

causeAndEffect.cause: An operator deploys 10 GB300 NVL72 racks without assessing structural loading, placing them on the existing 150 psf raised floor. The racks are positioned on standard floor tiles without slab-to-slab mounting.
causeAndEffect.effect: Each rack at 3,200 lbs on four levelling feet creates a point load of approximately 2,000 psf — over 13 times the rated floor capacity. Within 6 months, raised floor tiles crack and deflect. Coolant piping connections experience stress from floor movement, causing a slow leak at a manifold joint. The leak triggers an emergency shutdown of the 10-rack zone, resulting in 72 hours of downtime, €45,000 in coolant replacement and cleanup, and a structural remediation project costing €180,000 for slab-to-slab mounting of all 10 positions.
causeAndEffect.insight: Structural assessment is not optional for GPU racks above 60 kW. The point load calculation must be performed per EN 1991-1-1 before rack placement. Slab-to-slab mounting (bypassing the raised floor entirely) is the standard approach for liquid-cooled GPU racks. This must be included in the retrofit design scope from the outset — refer DC-AI-003 for detailed structural treatment.

logic.constraintLesson: The fundamental constraint at Level 6 is that GB300-class density creates cascading second-order effects that do not exist at 100 kW. The power delivery system must handle 155 kW peak per rack (not 135 kW average). The structural system must support 3,000–3,500 lbs per rack position. The cooling system must reject 35% more heat than at 100 kW. The electrical distribution must carry 250A per rack at 415V. And the facility must plan for the next generation (Rubin, 400+ kW) within 3 years. Each of these constraints interacts with the others — a floor reinforcement project may require draining the coolant loop, which requires thermal protection for adjacent live racks. The complexity is not linear with density — it is exponential.

weakestLink.whatsWrong: The most dangerous assumption is that GB300 deployment is simply 'more of the same' as 100 kW DLC — just bigger pipes and more CDUs. This linear thinking ignores the qualitative differences: power sloshing creates transient current spikes, structural loads exceed standard raised floor ratings, and the 50V DC internal busbar is at its engineering limit.
weakestLink.whatsRight: GB300 is a step change, not an increment. The 35% increase from 100 kW to 135 kW TDP triggers threshold effects in structural loading (exceeds 150 psf floor rating), electrical distribution (250A per rack requires dedicated 3-phase feeds), and future-proofing (800V DC transition imminent). Each threshold requires a discrete investment decision, not a proportional scaling of existing infrastructure.
howToGetThere: Step 1: Perform structural assessment at every planned GB300 rack position — calculate point load and compare against floor rating. At 3,000–3,500 lbs on 0.72 m², slab-to-slab mounting is almost certainly required (indicative: €3,000–8,000 per rack position for structural work, Irish market 2025)
Step 2: Size electrical feeds for peak (155 kW), not TDP (135 kW) — each rack requires approximately 250A at 415V 3-phase. Verify sub-distribution MCCB ratings and cable sizing for this current
Step 3: Size CDU and piping for peak heat rejection — 155 kW × rack count, not 135 kW × rack count. DN125 piping instead of DN100 if deploying more than 30 racks

Step 4: Plan cable and piping pathways for 800V DC transition — the Rubin platform (2027) will require different power distribution. Ensure pathways are accessible without disrupting the GB300 deployment

Step 5: Commission a thermal transient analysis modelling the effect of power sloshing on the cooling loop — CDU response time must be faster than the thermal time constant of the compute trays (indicative: included in CDU commissioning scope)

rhetoric.asset_management: Ten GB300 racks at 135 kW consume 1.35 MW — 27% of Clonshaugh's entire 5 MVA MIC. The revenue per rack at AI premium pricing is extraordinary: €150–200/kW/month × 135 kW = €20,250–27,000/month per rack = €243K–324K per rack per year. Ten racks: €2.4M–3.2M annual revenue from 10 rack positions that previously generated €172K/year at 8 kW. That is a 14–19× revenue multiplier per rack position. The capex required to serve these 10 positions is significant (approximately €2–3M for power, cooling, and structural combined), but the payback is under 12 months.
rhetoric.technology: The GB300's 800mm × 1200mm cabinet does not fit our existing 600mm × 1000mm row spacing. Aisle widths need re-evaluation. The 160A IEC 60309 power connectors require dedicated radial feeds from the sub-distribution — our existing whip infrastructure is 32A and cannot be re-used. The 90%/10% liquid/air heat split means we still need some air movement for OSFP transceivers and M.2 drives — the hall cannot be sealed to contain only liquid cooling. This is a ground-up infrastructure redesign within the existing building envelope.
rhetoric.technical: Electrical measurement for 10 GB300 racks: peak demand 10 × 155 kW = 1,550 kW = 1.55 MW. At 415V 3-phase and 0.85 pf: I = 1,550,000 / (415 × 1.732 × 0.85) = 2,537A. The Hall B sub-distribution board fed via 630A MCCBs cannot serve this load on a single circuit. A dedicated sub-distribution board with 3,200A bus section and individual 250A MCCB per rack is required. Transformer loading: current site at 85% + 1.55 MW additional = exceeds 5 MVA MIC. Grid connection upgrade is triggered — 12–24 month process via ESB Networks.
rhetoric.compliance: GB300 deployment at 10 racks adds 1.35 MW IT load. With DLC at PUE contribution 1.08, total facility power increase is approximately 1.46 MW. CRU/2025236 renewable obligation: 80% of the additional load must be from renewable sources. The existing CPPA covers 2.4 MW. Additional 1.46 MW requires either CPPA extension or REGO procurement. Failure to secure renewable sourcing means the facility cannot report Taxonomy alignment for the expanded capacity — impacting SFDR fund classification.
rhetoric.cost: GB300-specific infrastructure for 10 racks (indicative, Irish market 2025): Structural reinforcement (slab-to-slab mounting, 10 positions): €50K–80K. Dedicated 3,200A sub-distribution board: €85K–120K. 10 × 250A radial feeds with cable and containment: €150K–200K. Additional CDU capacity (2 × €130K): €260K. Piping modification for higher flow: €120K. Electrical supply for additional CDU: €45K. Commissioning and testing: €95K. Contingency 15%: €128K. Total: approximately €980K–1.05M for the GB300-specific increment above the base DLC infrastructure. This is ON TOP of the L5 DLC infrastructure — it is the premium for going from 100 kW to 135 kW density.

scenario.title: GB300 NVL72 Deployment Feasibility at Clonshaugh
scenario.situation: A hyperscaler tenant requests deployment of 20 GB300 NVL72 racks in Clonshaugh Hall B. Each rack: 135 kW typical, 155 kW peak, 3,200 lbs, 800mm × 1200mm footprint, 90% liquid / 10% air cooling. The facility has an existing DLC infrastructure designed for 100 kW/rack (L5 specification), 5 MVA MIC at 85% utilisation, 150 psf raised floor, and 3.2m ceiling height.
scenario.challenge: What infrastructure gaps must be closed to deploy 20 GB300 racks, and what is the total incremental investment above the existing DLC infrastructure?
scenario.fix: Step 1: Power — 20 × 155 kW peak = 3.1 MW. Current MIC headroom: 750 kVA (≈640 kW). Shortfall: approximately 2.46 MW. MIC upgrade to 8+ MVA required — ESB Networks process, 12–24 months, cost >€2M (T3). This is the critical path item. Step 2: Structural — 20 racks at 3,200 lbs each on 150 psf floor: point load approximately 2,000 psf, far exceeding rating. Slab-to-slab mounting required for all 20 positions: approximately €120K–160K. Step 3: Cooling — existing CDU system at 5 MW capacity must be increased to 20 × 135 kW = 2.7 MW for the GB300 zone (total including existing: 7.7 MW). Additional CDUs and piping: approximately €520K–650K. Step 4: Electrical distribution — 20 × 250A feeds require a new dedicated switchboard section and bus section upgrade: approximately €200K–280K. Step 5: Cabinet dimensions — 800mm × 1200mm footprint vs existing 600mm × 1000mm rack row layout. Aisle widths and row spacing must be reconfigured: civil works approximately €80K–120K. Step 6: Total incremental investment above existing DLC: approximately €3.1M–3.4M (excluding MIC upgrade). Including MIC upgrade (€2M+): approximately €5.1M–5.4M. Timeline: 18–24 months (constrained by MIC upgrade). Revenue at €200/kW/month: 20 × 135 × €200 × 12 = €6.48M/year. Payback on total investment: under 12 months if MIC upgrade completes on schedule.

title: Power Distribution Redesign
subtitle: Why existing switchboards, busbars, and PDUs can't serve AI racks
plainEnglish: Every component in the power chain — from the main switchboard bus section through to the rack PDU — was designed for 5–8 kW per rack. At AI density, every single component needs to be either replaced or upgraded. This is not a repair; it is a redesign of the entire electrical distribution system within the building.
retrofitRelevance: Power distribution redesign is typically the most expensive and most disruptive element of an AI retrofit — it requires working on live electrical systems in an occupied facility, with zero tolerance for outage in adjacent zones.
crossRefs: DC-LEARN-001 L4 (Power distribution topology)
DC-LEARN-005 L3 (UPS capacity constraints)
DC-AI-004 L1 (MIC utilisation — upstream constraint on distribution redesign)

facts.definition: The maximum continuous current a switchboard bus section can safely carry, expressed in amperes. Determined by conductor cross-section, material (copper or aluminium), enclosure ventilation, and ambient temperature. Governed by EN 61439-2:2011 §10 temperature rise limits.
facts.plain: The maximum electrical current the copper bars inside the switchboard can handle before they overheat. At Clonshaugh, the Hall B bus section is rated at 1,600A. At 415V and 0.85 power factor, this carries approximately 1.1 MW — nowhere near enough for an AI zone requiring 2–6 MW.
facts.number: Clonshaugh Hall B: 1,600A bus section at 415V 3-phase, 0.85 pf = approximately 1.1 MW deliverable. AI zone at 50 racks × 40 kW = 2.0 MW required. At 135 kW GB300 density: 20 racks × 135 kW = 2.7 MW. Bus section must be upgraded to 4,000A+ for the AI zone.
facts.standard: EN 61439-2:2011, Low-voltage switchgear and controlgear assemblies — Part 2: Power switchgear and controlgear assemblies, §10 Temperature rise verification
facts.soWhat: The bus section is the single most common binding constraint in AI retrofit power distribution — it determines the maximum power that can flow from the transformer to the distribution boards. Upgrading it typically requires a new switchboard section, which means planned outage and coordination with existing live circuits.

facts.definition: The arrangement of protective devices (MCCBs, fuses, relays) so that a fault is cleared by the device nearest to the fault, without tripping upstream devices that protect other circuits. IS 10101:2020+AC2:2025 §536 requires discrimination analysis for all installations.
facts.plain: If a single AI rack has an electrical fault, only that rack's circuit breaker should trip — not the breaker feeding the entire row, or worse, the entire hall. Ensuring this happens requires careful calculation of how each breaker responds to fault currents, and the calculation changes completely when you move from 32A rack circuits to 250A rack circuits.
facts.number: At 32A per rack (legacy), fault current discrimination between rack MCCB and row MCCB is straightforward — the current ratios are large. At 250A per rack (GB300), the discrimination margin narrows significantly because rack-level fault currents approach the row-level MCCB trip threshold. A formal discrimination study is required per IS 10101:2020+AC2:2025 §536.
facts.standard: IS 10101:2020+AC2:2025, §536 Co-ordination of protective devices. EN 61439-2:2011 §10.11 Short-circuit withstand strength.
facts.soWhat: A protection coordination failure at AI density means a single rack fault could trip the entire AI zone — cascading downtime across 20–50 racks instead of isolating a single rack. The financial impact: a 1-hour outage of a 50-rack GB300 zone at €200/kW/month lease rate represents approximately €925K of annualised SLA exposure.

facts.definition: The selection of cable conductor cross-section based on the continuous current demand of the circuit, ambient temperature, installation method, grouping, and voltage drop limits. IS 10101:2020+AC2:2025 §523 and Appendix 4 provide the tables and correction factors.
facts.plain: The thickness of copper wire needed to safely carry the electrical current to each rack. At 8 kW (legacy 32A), a standard 6mm² cable is adequate. At 135 kW (GB300, approximately 250A), you need 95–120mm² cable — roughly the thickness of a garden hose. These cables are heavy, rigid, and require much larger cable trays and containment than existing infrastructure provides.
facts.number: Legacy 32A circuit: 6mm² copper, weight approximately 0.1 kg/m. GB300 250A circuit: 120mm² copper (4 cores + earth), weight approximately 2.8 kg/m. For 20 racks at 15m average run: 20 × 15m × 2.8 kg/m = 840 kg of cable. Cable tray must be sized for this additional load.
facts.standard: IS 10101:2020+AC2:2025, §523 Current-carrying capacities, Appendix 4 (cable rating tables)
facts.soWhat: The cable infrastructure for AI racks is 25–30× heavier than legacy cabling per circuit. Existing cable trays, floor voids, and overhead containment are unlikely to have capacity for this additional weight and volume. Cable pathway redesign is almost always required.

facts.definition: A prefabricated, modular electrical distribution system using rigid copper or aluminium conductors in a sealed housing, mounted overhead or under the raised floor. An alternative to traditional cable distribution for high-density environments. Referenced in EN 61439-6:2012 for busbar trunking systems.
facts.plain: Instead of running heavy individual cables to each rack, a busbar trunking system is a pre-built electrical highway that runs along the ceiling above the rack row. Individual racks tap off the busbar through plug-in units. It is cleaner, more flexible, and easier to reconfigure than traditional cable runs — but significantly more expensive to install initially.
facts.number: Busbar trunking at 3,200A capacity: approximately €800–1,200/m installed (indicative, Irish market 2025). For a 50-rack row at 30m length: €24,000–36,000 per row. Versus traditional cable: 50 × 120mm² cable runs × 15m average × €85/m = €63,750 per row. Busbar is 40–60% cheaper at high density where cable volume becomes unmanageable.
facts.standard: EN 61439-6:2012, Low-voltage switchgear and controlgear assemblies — Part 6: Busbar trunking systems
facts.soWhat: At AI density, busbar trunking becomes cost-competitive with cable because the cable cross-sections are so large that cable management becomes impractical. Busbar also enables faster redeployment — racks can be moved or replaced without re-cabling, which matters when GPU hardware refreshes every 2–3 years.

facts.definition: The reduction in voltage between the supply point and the load, caused by current flowing through the resistance and reactance of the distribution conductors. IS 10101:2020+AC2:2025 §525 limits total voltage drop to 5% from the origin of the installation to the final load.
facts.plain: Electricity loses voltage as it travels through cables — like water pressure dropping in a long garden hose. The more current flowing, the bigger the drop. At 250A per rack, voltage drop over a 20m cable run can exceed the 5% limit allowed by Irish wiring rules, causing equipment to malfunction or refuse to power on.
facts.number: Voltage drop at 250A over 20m in 120mm² copper: approximately 3.8V per phase (0.9%). Total from origin: add transformer regulation (2–3%) and switchboard drop (0.5–1%) = total approximately 4–5%. Just within IS 10101 5% limit. At 30m cable run: total rises to approximately 5.5–6% — exceeds limit, requiring either larger cable or shorter runs.
facts.standard: IS 10101:2020+AC2:2025, §525 Voltage drop, limiting values
facts.soWhat: Voltage drop constrains the maximum distance between sub-distribution and rack. At AI density, the sub-distribution board may need to be placed within the hall, not in a remote electrical room — requiring space that competes with rack positions.

grammar.whatItLooksLike: New switchboard sections being installed adjacent to existing boards, with temporary barriers protecting live equipment. Heavy-gauge cables or busbar trunking being routed through overhead containment. Electricians working in controlled zones with permits to work. Discrimination study printouts pinned to the electrical room wall. New floor-standing sub-distribution boards appearing within the data hall, consuming rack positions.
siteChecklist: Record the bus section rating of the main switchboard and every sub-distribution board feeding the AI zone — compare against calculated demand at target density
Commission a protection coordination (discrimination) study for the redesigned distribution at target density — mandatory per IS 10101:2020+AC2:2025 §536
Calculate cable sizing for each rack feed at peak demand (155 kW for GB300) per IS 10101:2020+AC2:2025 §523, including derating for grouping and ambient temperature
Assess cable pathway capacity — can existing trays, floor voids, and containment accommodate the weight and volume of high-density cables?
Evaluate busbar trunking vs cable for the AI zone — obtain budgetary quotations for both options

causeAndEffect.cause: A retrofit project upgrades the CDU plant, piping, and rack manifolds for DLC but does not address the electrical distribution, assuming the existing switchboard and cabling can handle the additional load because the MIC upgrade has been approved.
causeAndEffect.effect: On go-live, the first 10 racks are energised successfully. When rack 11 is powered on, the sub-distribution MCCB feeding the row trips on overload — the 630A MCCB cannot carry 11 × 250A = 2,750A. All 11 racks lose power simultaneously. The cooling loop continues flowing, but without IT load, the coolant temperature drops below the dew point, causing condensation on warm surfaces. The entire zone must be de-energised, dried, and inspected before re-energisation — a 48-hour outage.
causeAndEffect.insight: Power distribution and cooling infrastructure must be designed and delivered in parallel, not sequentially. The tendency to prioritise the 'visible' liquid cooling work and defer the 'hidden' electrical work creates a go-live risk. Project management must track electrical distribution as a separate critical path item with its own commissioning milestones.

causeAndEffect.cause: An electrician sizes cables for the AI zone using IS 10101 Appendix 4 tables for current-carrying capacity but applies the standard grouping derating factor for legacy cable installations. In the AI zone, cables are grouped more densely in the tray due to larger cross-sections.
causeAndEffect.effect: The derating factor for grouping in IS 10101 reduces current-carrying capacity by 15–30% depending on the number of circuits. With 20 × 120mm² cables grouped in a single tray, the actual current-carrying capacity per cable is 20–25% less than the tabulated value. Under full load, cable operating temperature exceeds the 70°C or 90°C insulation limit, accelerating insulation degradation. Over 3–5 years, insulation breakdown occurs, causing an earth fault and zone-wide outage.
causeAndEffect.insight: Cable sizing for AI zones must account for the actual installation conditions — grouping, ambient temperature in the hot aisle containment zone, and installation method. The derating factors in IS 10101:2020+AC2:2025 Appendix 4 must be applied rigorously. A design review by an independent RECI-registered engineer is recommended before installation.

logic.constraintLesson: The fundamental constraint at Level 7 is that the entire electrical distribution system was designed as an integrated whole at the original rack density assumption. Changing the density at the rack level sends a shockwave backwards through every component: PDU → rack feed cable → sub-distribution MCCB → sub-distribution bus → main switchboard bus → transformer → grid connection. Each component has an independent capacity limit, and the lowest limit in the chain determines the maximum deliverable density. The redesign must address every link simultaneously — upgrading one without the others creates bottlenecks that are invisible until go-live.

weakestLink.whatsWrong: Project managers often focus on the grid connection (MIC upgrade) and the rack-level distribution (new PDUs) while overlooking the intermediate links — particularly the main switchboard bus section and the sub-distribution MCCBs. These components are physically inside existing equipment and are not visible in the way that cables and PDUs are.
weakestLink.whatsRight: The binding constraint is almost always in the middle of the chain — the bus section, the sub-distribution MCCBs, or the cable trays. These are the components that were sized exactly for the original load with minimal margin. The grid connection (MIC) is usually the most expensive to upgrade but has the longest lead time. The intermediate components are cheaper but require planned outage of adjacent circuits to replace — making them operationally complex.
howToGetThere: Step 1: Commission a full power distribution audit from transformer LV terminals to rack PDU for the AI zone — every component measured and rated (indicative: €15,000–25,000 for a 50-rack zone, Irish market 2025)
Step 2: Identify every component where rated capacity is less than the target demand — create a component-by-component upgrade schedule with cost and outage duration for each
Step 3: Commission the IS 10101 discrimination study for the redesigned distribution — this must be completed before ordering new MCCBs (indicative: €8,000–15,000)
Step 4: Evaluate busbar trunking vs cable for the rack feed distribution — at 40+ kW per rack, busbar is typically more cost-effective and more flexible

Step 5: Plan the electrical outage schedule — in a live facility, switchboard modifications require coordinated shutdowns of adjacent circuits. The schedule must be agreed with all tenants before work begins.

rhetoric.asset_management: The power distribution redesign for a 50-rack AI zone at 40 kW typically costs €800K–1.5M (indicative, Irish market 2025) — approximately 20–30% of the total retrofit budget. This is the line item that fund managers most often challenge because it appears to duplicate existing infrastructure. The existing infrastructure cannot deliver AI density — it is not a repair, it is a replacement necessitated by a 5–17× increase in per-rack demand. Attempting to re-use existing distribution to save capital creates a go-live risk that is far more expensive than the redesign.
rhetoric.technology: For Hall B at 40 kW/rack (50 racks, 2 MW), the redesigned distribution architecture would be: new 4,000A switchboard section (or busbar extension) from the transformer LV, new 1,600A sub-distribution boards within the hall (two boards, each serving 25 racks), busbar trunking at 3,200A along each row with 63A 3-phase tap-off per rack. This eliminates 50 individual cable runs and provides plug-and-play flexibility for rack moves during GPU hardware refresh cycles.
rhetoric.technical: Measured parameters for the Hall B distribution redesign: transformer available capacity after MIC upgrade = 3.5 MVA LV. Required bus section: P / (V × √3 × pf) = 2,000,000 / (415 × 1.732 × 0.85) = 3,274A minimum. Specify 4,000A for headroom. IS 10101 voltage drop: at 250A per rack and 15m average cable run in 120mm² copper, voltage drop = 0.87% per phase. Total from transformer: approximately 4.2% — within the 5% limit. Discrimination: 250A rack MCCB must discriminate with 1,600A sub-distribution MCCB — requires time-graded characteristics (0.1s rack, 0.3s sub-distribution) verified per EN 61439 and IS 10101 §536.
rhetoric.compliance: All electrical redesign work requires certification by a Registered Electrical Contractor (REC) under Safe Electric supervision. IS 10101:2020+AC2:2025 is the applicable standard — the entire redesigned distribution must comply with the current edition including the AC2:2025 corrigendum. A Completion Certificate must be issued for all new circuits. For the sub-distribution boards: EN 61439-2:2011 type-tested or partially type-tested assembly certification is required.
rhetoric.cost: Detailed power distribution redesign estimate for 50-rack AI zone at 40 kW (indicative, Irish market 2025): New 4,000A switchboard section: €120K–180K. Two in-hall sub-distribution boards at 1,600A: €90K–130K each. Busbar trunking (2 rows × 30m at €1,000/m): €60K. Alternatively, cable distribution (50 × 120mm² × 15m at €85/m installed): €64K. Protection coordination study: €12K. Cable tray and containment upgrades: €45K–65K. Commissioning, testing, and Safe Electric certification: €35K. Outage management and temporary supply: €25K. Total: approximately €500K–730K. At GB300 density (135 kW), scale to approximately €800K–1.2M.

scenario.title: Electrical Distribution Redesign for Hall B AI Zone
scenario.situation: Clonshaugh Hall B has approved a 50-rack AI zone at 40 kW/rack. The MIC upgrade to 8 MVA is underway (12 months remaining). The existing Hall B distribution consists of a 1,600A bus section feeding four 630A sub-distribution boards, each serving 50 racks via 32A single-phase whips. The facility operates 24/7 with live tenants in Hall A. Any electrical work in the main switchroom affects both halls.
scenario.challenge: How should the electrical distribution be redesigned to serve 2 MW to 50 rack positions without disrupting Hall A, and what is the outage plan for the switchboard modifications?
scenario.fix: Step 1: Install a new standalone 4,000A switchboard section in the electrical room, fed from the transformer via a new bus coupler — this avoids modifying the existing 1,600A section and eliminates outage risk to Hall A. Estimated cost: €150K–180K. Step 2: Run a new 1,600A busbar from the new section to two in-hall sub-distribution boards positioned at each end of the AI zone (estimated: €90K–130K per board plus €60K busbar). Step 3: Install overhead busbar trunking along each rack row, rated 3,200A, with 63A 3-phase plug-in tap-offs per rack position (estimated: €60K for 2 rows). Step 4: Commission IS 10101 discrimination study between rack MCCBs (63A), row busbar tap-offs, sub-distribution MCCBs (400A), and the 4,000A main incomer (estimated: €12K). Step 5: The only outage required is a 4-hour window to connect the new switchboard section to the transformer LV busbars — this is a controlled shutdown of the transformer, affecting both halls. Schedule for the lowest-load overnight window (typically Sunday 02:00–06:00) with all tenants notified 30 days in advance. UPS systems in both halls provide ride-through for the 4-hour window if battery capacity is adequate (verify: Clonshaugh UPS rated 2.8 MW with 10-minute autonomy — insufficient for 4 hours, therefore a temporary generator must be provisioned). Step 6: Total redesign cost: approximately €550K–730K. Timeline: 16 weeks from order to energisation (excluding MIC upgrade lead time).

title: Voltage Optimisation
subtitle: 415V vs 480V vs medium voltage to the rack — the efficiency argument
plainEnglish: At high density, the way electricity is delivered to the rack matters enormously. Higher voltage means lower current for the same power, which means thinner cables, less heat loss, and smaller switchboards. Europe uses 415V, the US uses 480V, and the next generation of AI hardware may bring medium voltage (800V DC) directly to the rack — a fundamental change in how data centres distribute power.
sourceNote: IS 10101:2020+AC2:2025 — T1. IEC 60364-1:2025 (DC provisions) — T1. Schneider Electric 800 VDC analysis (Oct 2025) — T3. NVIDIA GTC 2025 800V DC demonstration — T3.
retrofitRelevance: Voltage choice is a 15-year decision — it determines the entire electrical distribution architecture. A retrofit that chooses 415V 3-phase today must plan pathways for an 800V DC future, or face a complete re-wire within one GPU hardware generation.
crossRefs: DC-LEARN-001 L5 (Voltage and power factor fundamentals)
DC-LEARN-006 L3 (10 kV MV connection — upstream voltage context)
DC-AI-004 L3 (ESB Networks upgrade process — voltage-related)

facts.definition: The power dissipated as heat in an electrical conductor, proportional to the square of the current multiplied by the conductor resistance. At high rack densities, I²R losses in distribution conductors become a significant fraction of the total power budget.
facts.plain: Electricity generates heat as it flows through cables — and that wasted heat increases with the square of the current. Double the current, quadruple the loss. At 250A per rack (GB300 at 415V), a 20m cable run wastes approximately 1.5–2 kW as heat per rack — energy you pay for but that does zero computing.
facts.number: I²R loss at 250A in 120mm² copper, 20m: approximately 1.8 kW per rack. At 50 racks: 90 kW wasted as heat in cables alone. Annual cost: 90 × 8,760 × €0.12 = approximately €95,000/year. At 480V (208A for same power): loss drops to approximately 1.25 kW per rack — 31% reduction.
facts.standard: Electrical engineering first principles. IS 10101:2020+AC2:2025 §525 considers voltage drop which is directly related to I²R losses.
facts.soWhat: At AI density, I²R losses in distribution are no longer negligible — they can exceed 1% of the total IT load. Voltage optimisation is the primary lever to reduce these losses, because doubling voltage halves current and reduces I²R losses by 75%.

facts.definition: The standard low-voltage three-phase supply in Ireland and continental Europe, providing 415V between phases (240V phase-to-neutral). Derived from the 10 kV or 20 kV MV distribution network via step-down transformer.
facts.plain: The electricity voltage used in virtually all Irish commercial buildings — 415 volts between the three power lines. Every existing data centre in Ireland distributes power at 415V. It works well at legacy density but becomes increasingly inefficient at AI density because the current required to deliver 135 kW at 415V is enormous.
facts.number: At 135 kW (GB300), 415V 3-phase at 0.85 pf: I = 135,000 / (415 × 1.732 × 0.85) = 221A per rack. Conductor requirement: 95–120mm² copper. Compare with 480V: I = 191A (14% lower current, 27% lower I²R loss).
facts.standard: IS 10101:2020+AC2:2025 — standard supply voltage for Irish LV installations. IEC 60038:2009 Standard Voltages.
facts.soWhat: 415V is what Clonshaugh has and what every Irish electrician and equipment supplier is trained for. Switching to 480V or higher requires specialist equipment, different protection settings, and potentially different UPS systems — a significant practical barrier even when the efficiency argument favours higher voltage.

facts.definition: The standard low-voltage three-phase supply in North American data centres, providing 480V between phases (277V phase-to-neutral). Approximately 15.7% higher voltage than the European 415V standard.
facts.plain: American data centres use 480V instead of Europe's 415V — a seemingly small difference that delivers meaningful efficiency gains at high density. The higher voltage means 14% less current for the same power, which means smaller cables, less heat loss, and smaller switchboards. Some European facilities are now installing 480V zones specifically for AI racks.
facts.number: At 135 kW, 480V: I = 191A vs 221A at 415V. Cable can be 70mm² instead of 120mm². I²R loss reduction: approximately 27%. Annual saving for 50 racks: approximately €25,000 in distribution losses. Equipment cost premium for 480V in Irish market: approximately 10–15% due to non-standard procurement.
facts.standard: ANSI/NFPA 70 (US National Electrical Code). Not native to IS 10101 — requires engineering assessment for Irish installation.
facts.soWhat: 480V is an incremental improvement — meaningful savings at scale but requires non-standard equipment in the Irish market. The real efficiency step-change comes at 800V DC, which NVIDIA is pushing for the Rubin generation.

facts.definition: An emerging power distribution architecture where medium-voltage DC (800V) is delivered directly to the rack, eliminating multiple AC-DC-AC conversion stages. Demonstrated by NVIDIA at GTC 2025 as a sidecar PSU for the Rubin platform (expected 2027).
facts.plain: Instead of converting electricity from AC to DC multiple times on its journey from the grid to the GPU — each conversion wasting 1–3% — deliver 800 volts of DC power directly to the rack. At 135 kW, 800V DC means only 169 amps instead of 221 amps at 415V AC. At future densities of 400 kW+ per rack, 800V DC is the only viable approach because 415V AC would require over 600A per rack — physically impractical.
facts.number: At 400 kW/rack (Rubin Ultra projection): 415V AC current = 657A per rack (impractical). 800V DC current = 500A per rack (manageable). Efficiency gain: eliminating 2 conversion stages saves approximately 4–6% of total power — at 400 kW, that is 16–24 kW saved per rack.
facts.standard: NVIDIA GTC 2025 demonstration (T3). Schneider Electric 800 VDC analysis (Oct 2025 — T3). IEC 60364-1:2025 expanded DC provisions — T1.
facts.soWhat: 800V DC is not available for production deployment today — it targets 2027 with the Rubin platform. But any retrofit designed today must plan cable pathways and electrical room space for the transition. The cost of retrofitting cable routes after the fact is 3–5× the cost of planning them upfront.

facts.definition: The cumulative efficiency of all power conversion stages from the utility supply to the IT load. Each conversion (MV to LV, AC to DC, DC to lower DC) introduces losses. The fewer conversions, the higher the overall efficiency.
facts.plain: Every time electricity is converted from one form to another — from high voltage to low voltage, from AC to DC — a few percent is lost as heat. A traditional data centre might have 4–5 conversion steps between the grid and the GPU, losing 8–15% of the total power to conversion heat. Direct 800V DC to the rack eliminates 2–3 of these steps.
facts.number: Traditional chain: MV→LV transformer (98%) → UPS AC-DC-AC (94–96%) → PDU transformer (98%) → server PSU AC-DC (92–95%) = cumulative 82–87%. Optimised 800V DC chain: MV→800V DC (97%) → rack PSU DC-DC (98%) = cumulative approximately 95%. Improvement: 8–13 percentage points.
facts.standard: Schneider Electric 800 VDC analysis (Oct 2025 — T3). EN 50600-2-2:2019 §6 power efficiency considerations.
facts.soWhat: At 50 racks × 135 kW, a 10% efficiency improvement saves approximately 675 kW of continuous load — €710,000/year at €0.12/kWh. This is the strongest long-term argument for planning toward 800V DC, even though current GPU hardware cannot use it yet.

grammar.whatItLooksLike: Electrical design drawings showing voltage levels at each stage of the distribution chain. Comparison tables of cable sizes at 415V vs 480V vs 800V DC. Empty conduit runs or oversized cable trays labelled 'future 800V DC pathway'. Transformer nameplates showing secondary voltage taps. UPS output voltage selector settings. Engineers debating whether to install 480V zones now or wait for 800V DC.
siteChecklist: Record the voltage at every point in the distribution chain from transformer secondary to rack PDU — identify all conversion stages and their individual efficiencies
Calculate I²R losses in the existing distribution at target density — what percentage of total power is lost in conductors?
Assess whether the transformer has 480V tap capability — many European transformers can be reconfigured for 480V secondary
Plan physical pathways (cable trays, risers, floor penetrations) that can accommodate future 800V DC distribution without requiring structural modification
Confirm UPS compatibility with the planned voltage — most modern UPS systems support 380–480V input and output

causeAndEffect.cause: A retrofit project installs 415V distribution for a 50-rack AI zone at 135 kW/rack, using 120mm² cables for each rack feed. The design does not include any provision for future voltage upgrade because 800V DC is 'not yet available' and the project team focuses on delivering to today's requirement.
causeAndEffect.effect: Three years later, the tenant upgrades to Rubin-class hardware at 400 kW/rack. At 415V 3-phase, each rack now requires 657A — the 120mm² cables are inadequate (rated approximately 300A). The entire cable infrastructure must be replaced. Additionally, the cable trays cannot physically accommodate the 300mm² cables that 657A would require. The cable pathway itself must be rebuilt. Total cost of the voltage-agnostic rework: approximately €1.5–2.5M. Had the original design included 800V DC pathways (oversized conduits, cable tray capacity, electrical room space), the incremental cost at original build would have been approximately €150K–250K.
causeAndEffect.insight: Future-proofing for voltage change does not mean installing 800V equipment today — it means ensuring that the physical pathways (conduits, trays, risers, floor penetrations) and electrical room space can accommodate the transition without demolition. This is a low-cost, high-value insurance policy.

causeAndEffect.cause: An operator deploys a 480V zone in an Irish data centre for AI racks, achieving 14% lower distribution current. However, the UPS system was not verified for 480V output capability and remains at 415V, requiring a step-up transformer between UPS and the 480V zone.
causeAndEffect.effect: The step-up transformer introduces a 1.5–2% additional conversion loss — consuming approximately half the efficiency gain that 480V was supposed to deliver. The transformer also occupies 3m² of floor space, generates heat requiring additional cooling, and adds a single point of failure to the UPS protection chain. Net efficiency gain: approximately 7% instead of 14%. The 480V decision was correct but the implementation was incomplete.
causeAndEffect.insight: Voltage optimisation must be considered as a system-level change, not a component-level change. Every element in the chain — transformer, UPS, PDU, switchgear, cable — must be verified for the new voltage. Mixing voltages within the chain introduces conversion stages that can negate the efficiency benefit.

logic.constraintLesson: The fundamental constraint at Level 8 is the tension between optimising for today's technology and planning for tomorrow's. 415V is the path of least resistance — standard equipment, standard skills, standard procurement. 480V offers meaningful efficiency gains but non-standard procurement in the Irish market. 800V DC offers transformative efficiency but is not yet available for production deployment. The rational strategy is to deploy at 415V or 480V today while designing the physical infrastructure (pathways, space, structural supports) to accommodate 800V DC within 3 years. The cost of physical future-proofing is minimal; the cost of not future-proofing is a complete re-wire.

weakestLink.whatsWrong: The most common error is treating voltage as a fixed parameter rather than a design variable. Operators inherit 415V because that is what the building has, and never evaluate alternatives — even when a new switchboard and distribution is being installed specifically for the AI zone.
weakestLink.whatsRight: When installing new distribution for an AI zone, the voltage is a choice — not a given. The new switchboard, cables, and PDUs are all being procured specifically for this zone. At this point, specifying 480V equipment adds approximately 10–15% to procurement cost but delivers 27% lower I²R losses — a net positive over any lease term longer than 3 years. And regardless of today's voltage choice, oversizing pathways for 800V DC adds 5–8% to civil works cost and eliminates the largest future risk.
howToGetThere: Step 1: Calculate the total conversion efficiency chain for the current distribution and for each voltage option (415V, 480V, 800V DC) — quantify the annual energy and cost difference

Step 2: Verify transformer secondary voltage tap capability — if 480V is available, the distribution redesign should evaluate it as the base case (cost: nil for verification, part of the distribution audit)

Step 3: Specify cable trays and conduits at 150% of current requirement — the additional 50% provides capacity for either higher-gauge cables or additional circuits during the 800V DC transition (indicative cost premium: 5–8% of civil works)
Step 4: Reserve 6–8m² in the electrical room for future 800V DC conversion equipment — this space costs nothing to reserve now but would require demolition to create later

rhetoric.asset_management: The voltage decision determines the facility's efficiency floor for the next 15 years. At 415V with 50 GB300 racks, annual distribution losses are approximately €95,000. At 480V: approximately €69,000 — saving €26,000/year. Over a 12-year lease: €312,000 saving. The 480V equipment premium: approximately €80,000–120,000. Net benefit: approximately €190K–232K over the lease. And if the tenant transitions to 800V DC hardware (Rubin, 2027), the physical pathways are already in place — no additional civil works required.
rhetoric.technology: For the Hall B AI zone, my recommendation is 415V for the initial deployment (lowest procurement risk, fastest delivery, no specialist equipment needed) with three future-proofing provisions: (1) cable trays at 150% capacity, (2) electrical room space reserved for 800V DC conversion equipment, (3) conduit routes pre-installed but empty between the electrical room and the rack zone. These three provisions add approximately €120K to the project but eliminate the €1.5–2.5M rework cost if we transition to 800V DC within 5 years.
rhetoric.technical: Comparative I²R loss analysis for 50 racks at 135 kW, 20m average cable run: 415V 3-phase (221A/rack, 120mm² Cu): I²R = 221² × 0.153 × 10⁻³ × 20 × 3 = 1,340W per rack. 480V 3-phase (191A/rack, 95mm² Cu): I²R = 191² × 0.193 × 10⁻³ × 20 × 3 = 850W per rack. 800V DC (169A/rack, 70mm² Cu): I²R = 169² × 0.268 × 10⁻³ × 20 × 2 = 610W per rack. Note the DC calculation uses 2 conductors vs 3 for AC — a further advantage. Annual saving: 415→480: €26K; 415→800: €38K for 50 racks.
rhetoric.compliance: 480V installation in Ireland requires careful navigation of IS 10101:2020+AC2:2025 — the standard is written for 230/400V nominal but does not prohibit 480V. An engineering assessment must demonstrate that all protection devices, cable ratings, and isolation requirements are met at the non-standard voltage. This is a specialist design exercise. For 800V DC: IEC 60364-1:2025 (6th edition) has expanded DC provisions, providing a framework for DC distribution design. Irish adoption will depend on NSAI's implementation timeline for the updated IEC standard.
rhetoric.cost: Voltage option cost comparison for 50-rack AI zone at 135 kW (indicative, Irish market 2025): 415V baseline: switchboard €150K, cables €64K, trays €45K, sub-DBs €260K = €519K. 480V: switchboard €172K (+15%), cables €48K (smaller gauge), trays €45K, sub-DBs €299K (+15%) = €564K (+€45K). 480V with 800V future-proofing: add €120K for oversized pathways and reserved space = €684K. Recommendation: 415V with 800V future-proofing at €639K (€519K + €120K). The €120K insurance policy has a breakeven probability of approximately 65% — the probability that the facility will need to accommodate 400+ kW racks within 10 years. Given NVIDIA's roadmap, that probability is well above 65%.

scenario.title: Voltage Strategy for Hall B AI Zone
scenario.situation: Clonshaugh Hall B is being fitted with new electrical distribution for a 50-rack AI zone. The transformer has a 480V secondary tap available. Current facility distribution is 415V throughout. The tenant's initial deployment is GB300 at 135 kW/rack, with contractual option for 200+ kW hardware within 5 years. NVIDIA has demonstrated 800V DC for the Rubin platform (2027). The project budget has €100K contingency remaining.
scenario.challenge: Should the new AI zone distribution be installed at 415V, 480V, or planned for 800V DC — and what future-proofing provisions should be included regardless of the voltage choice?
scenario.fix: Step 1: Evaluate 480V — transformer has the tap, saving is €26K/year in distribution losses. Equipment procurement at 480V adds approximately €45K. Payback: 21 months. However, IS 10101 compliance at 480V requires a specialist engineering assessment (€8K–12K) and non-standard protection device procurement (4–6 week additional lead time). Step 2: Evaluate 415V with future-proofing — standard procurement, standard skills, no compliance risk. Add three provisions: cable trays at 150% width (€35K premium), reserved electrical room space (€15K for partition and labelling), pre-installed empty conduits for future 800V DC (€70K). Total: €120K future-proofing. Step 3: Risk assessment — probability of needing 800V DC within 10 years: estimated 70%+ based on NVIDIA Rubin roadmap. Cost of pathway retrofit without pre-installed provisions: €1.5–2.5M. Expected value of not future-proofing: 70% × €2M = €1.4M. Expected value of future-proofing: €120K certain cost. The insurance policy is rational by a factor of 10:1. Step 4: Recommendation — install at 415V (fastest, lowest risk, standard compliance) with the €120K future-proofing package. This fits within the €100K contingency plus €20K budget adjustment. The 480V option is technically sound but adds procurement risk and compliance overhead that is not justified when the real efficiency step-change comes with 800V DC in 3 years.

title: The Retrofit Investment Decision
subtitle: Should Clonshaugh Hall B go to 40 kW, 100 kW, or not bother?
plainEnglish: This is where everything comes together. The physics, the costs, the regulations, the grid constraints, the structural limits, the tenant demand, and the fund returns — all converge on a single question: is this retrofit worth doing, and at what density? Five experienced professionals look at the same data and reach different conclusions, depending on what they are optimising for.
sourceNote: All sources from L1–L8 converge here. EN 50600-1:2019 §5 business impact analysis — T1. CRU/2025236 — T1. EU Taxonomy Delegated Act 2021/2139 — T1.
retrofitRelevance: This is the module's purpose — the investment decision that justifies or rejects the retrofit. Every preceding level exists to inform this decision with defensible, sourced data.
crossRefs: DC-AI-005 L9 (Residual value & exit strategy)
DC-AI-006 L1 (EU Taxonomy PUE — regulatory constraint on the decision)
DC-AI-007 L1 (AI Readiness Assessment methodology — the decision process)
DC-AI-008 L9 (Investment Decision Framework — market context)

facts.definition: The present value of all future cash flows (revenue, energy savings, operating costs) minus the initial capital investment, discounted at the fund's required rate of return. The primary financial metric for evaluating infrastructure investment decisions.
facts.plain: A way to compare the cost of the retrofit today against the money it will generate over its lifetime, accounting for the fact that money in the future is worth less than money today. A positive NPV means the retrofit creates value; a negative NPV means it destroys value. The fund's hurdle rate determines how aggressively future cash flows are discounted.
facts.number: Clonshaugh Hall B 50-rack DLC retrofit at 40 kW: capex approximately €5M (power + cooling + structural). Revenue uplift: approximately €2.5M/year (AI premium minus existing revenue). At 7% discount rate over 12-year lease: NPV approximately €12.5M. At 100 kW: capex approximately €8M, revenue approximately €5.5M/year, NPV approximately €28M. Sensitivity: NPV turns negative if rack fill rate drops below 35% at 40 kW or 25% at 100 kW.
facts.standard: EN 50600-1:2019 §5 — business impact analysis methodology. Financial modelling standards (RICS/SCSI valuation practice).
facts.soWhat: The NPV analysis shows that the retrofit is strongly value-creating at both 40 kW and 100 kW density — the critical variable is not the density choice but the fill rate. A half-empty AI zone at any density destroys value faster than a full legacy zone.

facts.definition: The probability that the AI zone will not achieve the planned rack occupancy within the projected timeline, resulting in revenue below forecast while the fixed infrastructure costs are already committed.
facts.plain: You have built a 50-rack AI zone at enormous cost — but what if only 20 racks get filled? The power distribution, cooling system, and structural reinforcement cost nearly the same whether you fill 20 racks or 50. Every empty rack is burning depreciation and energy costs while generating zero revenue. Fill rate is the single biggest risk in any AI retrofit.
facts.number: At 50 racks / 100 kW, breakeven fill rate is approximately 25% (13 racks) at AI premium pricing. Below 13 racks, the retrofit NPV turns negative. Market data: average fill time for AI-ready colocation in European Tier 1 markets is 6–12 months for the first 50% of capacity (CBRE, JLL market reports 2025 — T2). Supply chain risk: if GPU hardware delivery is delayed, tenant cannot occupy — and the facility bears the cost.
facts.standard: Industry market data — CBRE, JLL colocation market reports (T2). Financial risk analysis per RICS/SCSI valuation practice.
facts.soWhat: Fill rate risk is the argument for phased deployment — build 25 racks of infrastructure first, fill them, then build the next 25. Phasing costs approximately 15% more in total (mobilisation twice) but halves the peak capital at risk.

facts.definition: A retrofit strategy where infrastructure is delivered in sequential phases, each sized for a portion of the total capacity. Reduces peak capital exposure and allows each phase to be informed by operational experience from the previous phase.
facts.plain: Instead of building the entire AI zone at once, build it in two or three stages. Phase 1 might be 25 racks with the power and cooling infrastructure to match. If those fill successfully, Phase 2 adds the next 25. This halves the initial investment and lets you learn from Phase 1 mistakes before repeating them at scale.
facts.number: Clonshaugh Phase 1 (25 racks at 40 kW): capex approximately €3.2M. Phase 2 (additional 25 racks): approximately €2.3M. Total phased: €5.5M vs single-phase €5.0M — 10% premium. But peak capital at risk: €3.2M vs €5.0M — 36% reduction in downside exposure.
facts.standard: EN 50600-1:2019 §5 — scalable infrastructure design. Industry best practice for colocation investment.
facts.soWhat: Phasing trades a 10% cost premium for a 36% reduction in capital at risk. For a fund managing multiple assets, the portfolio-level risk reduction is typically worth more than the per-project cost increase.

facts.definition: The economic value lost by not retrofitting — measured in lost tenant revenue, asset devaluation, and competitive positioning decline as the market shifts to AI-ready facilities.
facts.plain: Doing nothing is also a decision — and it has a cost. Every month without AI-ready capacity is a month where tenants are leaving for competitors who do offer it. The asset value declines as the market increasingly prices AI-readiness into data centre valuations. The opportunity cost of waiting is not zero — it is the revenue and asset value that erodes while you deliberate.
facts.number: Clonshaugh tenant attrition: 2 tenants have served notice due to lack of AI capacity (per Conor's voice sample). Annual revenue at risk: €2.4M. Asset devaluation: non-AI-ready facilities trading at 15–25% discount to AI-ready equivalents in European markets (CBRE, 2025 — T2). On a €40M asset valuation, that is €6–10M of value at risk from inaction.
facts.standard: CBRE European Data Centre Market Report 2025 — T2. RICS/SCSI valuation practice — market comparison approach.
facts.soWhat: The decision is not 'retrofit or save money' — it is 'retrofit or lose money'. The opportunity cost of inaction exceeds the retrofit capex within 2–3 years at current market pricing. The only rational reason not to retrofit is if the facility cannot physically accommodate AI density (structural, grid, or lease constraints) — not because the investment case is weak.

facts.definition: The choice of target rack density for the retrofit — typically between 40 kW/rack (minimum viable AI), 100 kW/rack (current production standard), and 135+ kW/rack (GB300-class). Each scenario has different capex, revenue, risk, and future-proofing characteristics.
facts.plain: Choosing how much power to deliver to each rack is the most consequential decision in the retrofit — it determines every downstream engineering choice and its cost. Going to 40 kW is the cheapest entry point but may be outgrown in 3 years. Going to 100 kW costs more but serves the current market. Going to 135 kW costs the most but accommodates GB300-class hardware immediately.
facts.number: Clonshaugh Hall B 50-rack scenarios: 40 kW: capex ~€5M, revenue ~€2.5M/yr, NPV €12.5M. 100 kW: capex ~€8M, revenue ~€5.5M/yr, NPV €28M. 135 kW: capex ~€10.5M, revenue ~€7.2M/yr, NPV €34M. All scenarios assume 80% fill at year 2. Sensitivity: at 50% fill, only the 100 kW and 135 kW scenarios maintain positive NPV.
facts.standard: Financial modelling — indicative, based on T2/T3 market data and Clonshaugh parameters.
facts.soWhat: Higher density has higher capex but disproportionately higher revenue — the revenue-per-euro-of-capex ratio improves with density because the building infrastructure (walls, roof, security) is fixed cost. The risk is fill rate — higher density means higher-value tenants who are fewer in number.

grammar.whatItLooksLike: A boardroom table with five professionals: Conor (fund), Helena (CTO), Eoin (MEP), Rachel (ESG), Padraig (QS). A projector showing the NPV model with sensitivity analysis. Marked-up floor plans of Hall B showing three density scenarios overlaid in different colours. Risk registers. CRU/2025236 compliance checklists. ESB Networks correspondence about MIC upgrade timelines. Each professional has a different recommendation — and all of them are defensible.
siteChecklist: Confirm tenant demand — is there a signed letter of intent or binding pre-lease for the AI zone?
Complete the power distribution audit (L7) and structural assessment (DC-AI-003) to establish the physical constraints on each density scenario
Model NPV for each density scenario (40/100/135 kW) at 50%, 80%, and 100% fill rates over the lease term
Confirm MIC upgrade timeline with ESB Networks — this is the critical path constraint for any scenario above current headroom
Assess CRU/2025236 renewable sourcing requirement for each scenario — is the CPPA adequate or does a new agreement need to be procured?

causeAndEffect.cause: An investment committee approves a 40 kW retrofit for Hall B based on current tenant demand, explicitly deferring the 100 kW option as 'gold-plating'. The retrofit is completed in 12 months. Within 18 months, the tenant requests 80 kW for next-generation hardware.
causeAndEffect.effect: The 40 kW infrastructure — CDUs, piping, switchboard, cables — cannot serve 80 kW without replacement. The facility must undergo a second retrofit while the first is still being depreciated. Total capex: €5M (40 kW) + €6M (upgrade to 100 kW, including demolition and re-routing) = €11M — versus €8M if 100 kW had been built initially. The tenant experiences 6 months of construction disruption during the upgrade, straining the relationship. The fund reports a €3M write-down on the 40 kW infrastructure.
causeAndEffect.insight: The decision between 40 kW and 100 kW is not about current demand — it is about the cost of being wrong. Building 40 kW when 100 kW was needed costs €3M+ in stranded infrastructure and disruption. Building 100 kW when 40 kW would have sufficed costs €3M in additional capex but the infrastructure is future-proofed. The downside of under-building is larger than the downside of over-building.

causeAndEffect.cause: A fund manager decides not to retrofit Hall B at all, reasoning that AI workloads represent a bubble and the facility can continue serving traditional compute tenants at 8 kW/rack for the remaining 12-year lease.
causeAndEffect.effect: Over the next 24 months, 3 additional tenants serve notice citing inability to support their evolving compute requirements. Annual revenue drops by €1.8M. The facility's market valuation declines by approximately 20% as comparable AI-ready facilities trade at premium multiples. The fund's total return for the investment period is reduced by approximately €12M in combined revenue loss and asset devaluation — exceeding the cost of any retrofit scenario by a factor of 2.
causeAndEffect.insight: The opportunity cost of inaction is not hypothetical — it is measurable in tenant attrition, revenue decline, and asset devaluation. Industry data shows that non-AI-ready facilities in European Tier 1 markets are trading at 15–25% valuation discounts (CBRE 2025). The decision not to retrofit is the most expensive option on the table.

logic.constraintLesson: The fundamental constraint at Level 9 is not engineering — every preceding level has demonstrated that the technical challenges of AI retrofit are solvable. The constraint is decisional. It is the tension between the certainty of capex today and the uncertainty of revenue tomorrow. It is the tension between the fund's quarterly reporting cadence and the 12–24 month infrastructure lead time. It is the tension between the individual asset decision and the portfolio strategy. The retrofit investment decision is ultimately a risk management exercise, not an engineering exercise. The engineering answers are already known (L1–L8). The question is whether the organisation is structured to act on them.

weakestLink.whatsWrong: The most common failure mode is analysis paralysis — commissioning ever-more-detailed studies while the market moves, tenants leave, and competitors build. The perfect study arrives 18 months late, confirming what the initial assessment showed: the retrofit is value-creating at any reasonable density, and the risk of inaction exceeds the risk of action.
weakestLink.whatsRight: The decision framework is simpler than it appears: (1) Is there demonstrated tenant demand or strong market indicators for AI density in this location? (2) Can the facility physically accommodate AI density (grid, structure, cooling)? (3) Does the NPV analysis show positive returns at realistic fill rates? If all three answers are yes, the decision is to proceed — the remaining variables (exact density, phasing, timeline) are optimisation questions, not go/no-go questions.
howToGetThere: Step 1: Commission the AI Readiness Screening (DC-AI-S01, €8,500) to confirm physical feasibility at the target density range — this answers question 2 in 2 weeks
Step 2: Validate tenant demand with binding LOI or market analysis from at least two independent brokers — this answers question 1
Step 3: Run the NPV model at 40/100/135 kW with sensitivity on fill rate, energy price, and density growth — if NPV is positive at 50% fill for any scenario, the project is viable
Step 4: Select density based on NPV maximisation at realistic fill rates — typically 100 kW provides the best risk-adjusted return because it serves the broadest range of tenants
Step 5: Approve Phase 1 (25 racks) to limit peak capital exposure while demonstrating operational capability — expand to Phase 2 when Phase 1 achieves 70%+ fill

rhetoric.asset_management: The board paper is clear: at any density above 40 kW with fill rates above 35%, the retrofit NPV is positive. At 100 kW with 80% fill, NPV is approximately €28M on €8M capex over a 12-year lease — a 3.5× return on capital. The risk is fill rate, and we mitigate that with phasing and pre-lease commitments. The risk of inaction is €12M+ in revenue loss and asset devaluation. I am recommending 100 kW, phased (25 racks Phase 1, 25 racks Phase 2), with Phase 1 capex of approximately €5M and a go/no-go gate for Phase 2 at 70% fill.
rhetoric.technology: My recommendation: deploy at 100 kW infrastructure with 135 kW-capable pathways. This means: DN125 piping (not DN100), 4,000A switchboard section (not 3,200A), cable trays at 150% capacity, and slab-to-slab structural reinforcement. The premium for this over-specification is approximately €800K on an €8M project — 10%. It means when the tenant moves to GB300-class hardware in 2–3 years, we do not need to touch the infrastructure. We add CDU capacity and rack-level distribution only.
rhetoric.technical: The binding constraint analysis for all three scenarios: At 40 kW (50 racks, 2 MW): MIC upgrade required (750 kVA headroom insufficient). Bus section replacement required (1,600A insufficient). Floor loading adequate. CDU: 4+1 at 500 kW. At 100 kW (50 racks, 5 MW): MIC upgrade to 10+ MVA. New dedicated transformer for AI zone. Floor: slab-to-slab mounting. CDU: 10+2. At 135 kW (20 racks, 2.7 MW): same MIC/transformer as 100 kW, fewer racks but higher per-rack infrastructure. All three scenarios trigger the same grid constraint — MIC upgrade is the critical path regardless of density choice. This means the decision between densities does not change the timeline, only the scope and cost of downstream infrastructure.
rhetoric.compliance: All three scenarios trigger CRU/2025236 renewable obligation for the additional load. At 40 kW (2 MW additional): CPPA extension or REGO procurement for 1.6 MW (80% of 2 MW). At 100 kW (5 MW additional): new CPPA for 4 MW. At 135 kW (2.7 MW additional, 20 racks): CPPA for 2.16 MW. EU Taxonomy alignment: only the DLC scenarios (all three) achieve PUE ≤1.3 for the AI zone. SFDR Article 8/9 fund reporting benefits from Taxonomy alignment. EED Article 12 annual reporting is mandatory regardless. The regulatory landscape favours the retrofit — doing nothing does not avoid regulatory obligations, it just reports worse numbers.
rhetoric.cost: Summary cost comparison (indicative, Irish market 2025): 40 kW / 50 racks: Power distribution €730K, cooling (DLC) €1.82M, structural €350K, grid (MIC upgrade) €2M, commissioning €250K, contingency €775K. Total: approximately €5.9M. 100 kW / 50 racks: Power €1.1M, cooling €2.43M, structural €550K, grid €2.5M, commissioning €380K, contingency €1.05M. Total: approximately €8.0M. 135 kW / 20 racks: Power €1.2M, cooling €1.65M, structural €250K, grid €2.5M, commissioning €280K, contingency €880K. Total: approximately €6.8M. Revenue (Year 2, 80% fill): 40 kW: €2.0M. 100 kW: €4.4M. 135 kW: €4.3M. Best risk-adjusted return: 100 kW at 50 racks — highest NPV, broadest tenant appeal, future-proofed to 135 kW with pathway provisions.

scenario.title: Investment Committee Decision — Clonshaugh Hall B
scenario.situation: Clonshaugh DC owner (fund) must decide: retrofit Hall B for AI, and at what density? Current state: 200 racks at 8 kW/rack, 5 MVA MIC at 85%, PUE 1.50, 12-year remaining lease, 2 tenants departing. Market: AI-ready premium €150–200/kW/month, 6–12 month fill for Tier 1 markets. Three proposals on the table: A) 40 kW/50 racks (€5.9M), B) 100 kW/50 racks (€8M), C) 135 kW/20 racks (€6.8M). Fund hurdle rate: 7%.
scenario.challenge: Which proposal maximises risk-adjusted NPV over the remaining lease, and what phasing strategy limits downside exposure?
scenario.fix: Step 1: NPV at 80% fill, 7% discount, 12 years — A: NPV €10.8M. B: NPV €27.6M. C: NPV €26.1M. All positive. Step 2: NPV at 50% fill — A: NPV €4.2M. B: NPV €14.1M. C: NPV €13.8M. All still positive. Step 3: Breakeven fill rate — A: 35%. B: 25%. C: 22%. Proposal B and C have lower breakeven — they are MORE resilient to poor fill, not less, because the revenue per rack is disproportionately higher. Step 4: Future-proofing — A locks out density growth above 40 kW without second retrofit. B at 100 kW with 135 kW pathway provisions costs €800K premium but eliminates future infrastructure rework. C serves only 20 rack positions, limiting total revenue. Step 5: Phasing — Proposal B phased: Phase 1 (25 racks, €5M), Phase 2 at 70% fill (25 racks, €3.5M). Peak capital at risk: €5M vs €8M single-phase. Phase 1 NPV standalone at 80% fill: €12.8M. Step 6: Recommendation — Proposal B, phased, with 135 kW pathway provisions. Approve Phase 1 (€5M) immediately, trigger Phase 2 at 70% fill. MIC upgrade application to ESB Networks: submit this week (12–24 month lead time is the critical path). CPPA extension: initiate negotiation for 4 MW additional renewable sourcing.
